label: 2 Relational Data Model and Relational Database Constraints.pptx

params.file: 2 Relational Data Model and Relational Database Constraints.pptx

title: Chapter 2 : The Relational Data Model and Relational Database Constraints

questions.text: Who is credited with first proposing the Relational Model of Data in 1970?
questions.explanation: Dr. E.F. Codd proposed the relational model in his 1970 paper 'A Relational Model for Large Shared Data Banks'.

questions.text: In the formal relational model, what is the informal equivalent of a 'Relation'?
questions.explanation: Informally, a relation looks like a table of values.

questions.text: What is the formal term for a 'Row' in the relational model?
questions.explanation: In the formal model, rows are called tuples.

questions.text: The column header in a table is formally referred to as a/an:
questions.explanation: In the formal model, the column header is called an attribute name (or just attribute).

questions.text: What type of key is used when row-ids or sequential numbers are assigned to identify rows in a table?
questions.explanation: Sequential numbers or row-ids assigned as keys are called artificial or surrogate keys.

questions.text: Which of the following describes a 'Relation Schema'?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The description of a relation, denoted by \( R(A_1, A_2, ..., A_n) \)

choices.label: C
choices.text: The current values stored in a table

choices.label: D
choices.text: The Cartesian product of all domains

questions.explanation: A schema is the description of a relation, consisting of its name and its attributes.

questions.text: In the tuple \( <632895, "John Smith", "Atlanta"> \), the fact it has 3 values makes it a:
questions.explanation: A tuple with \( n \) values is called an \( n \)-tuple.

questions.text: A domain has a logical definition and a:
questions.explanation: A domain has both a logical definition (e.g., USA phone numbers) and a data-type or format (e.g., (ddd)ddd-dddd).

questions.text: The formal definition of a relation state \( r(R) \) is a subset of the ________ of the domains of its attributes.
questions.explanation: The relation state is a subset of the Cartesian product of the domains of its attributes: \( r(R) \subseteq dom(A_1) \times dom(A_2) \times ... \times dom(A_n) \).

questions.text: If \( dom(A_1) = \{0, 1\} \) and \( dom(A_2) = \{a, b, c\} \), how many possible combinations are in the Cartesian product?
questions.explanation: The Cartesian product has \( 2 \times 3 = 6 \) combinations: \( \{<0,a>, <0,b>, <0,c>, <1,a>, <1,b>, <1,c>\} \).

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding the ordering of tuples in a relation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tuples must be ordered by the primary key.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tuples are not considered to be ordered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tuples are ordered chronologically by insertion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tuples must be ordered alphabetically by the first attribute.

questions.explanation: According to the characteristics of relations, tuples are not considered to be ordered, even if they appear so in a table.

questions.text: In the formal relational model, values in a tuple are considered to be:
questions.explanation: Values in a tuple are considered atomic (indivisible).

questions.text: What is the purpose of a 'null' value in the relational model?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To represent a value of zero

choices.label: B
choices.text: To represent unknown or inapplicable values

choices.label: C
choices.text: To act as a primary key

questions.explanation: A special null value is used for values that are unknown or inapplicable to certain tuples.

questions.text: How do we formally refer to the value of attribute \( A_i \) for tuple \( t \)?
questions.explanation: Component values are referred to by \( t[A_i] \) or \( t.A_i \).

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the three main types of integrity constraints?
questions.explanation: The three main types are Key, Entity integrity, and Referential integrity. Domain constraints are implicit.

questions.text: A set of attributes \( SK \) of \( R \) where no two tuples in any valid state have the same value for \( SK \) is called a:
questions.explanation: A superkey is a set of attributes that uniquely identifies each tuple in a relation.

questions.text: A 'minimal' superkey is formally called a:
questions.explanation: A key is a superkey such that removal of any attribute from it results in a set that is no longer a superkey (minimal uniqueness).

questions.text: If a relation has multiple candidate keys, the one chosen to uniquely identify tuples is the:
questions.explanation: One of the candidate keys is chosen arbitrarily to be the primary key.

questions.text: How is a Primary Key typically denoted in a relation schema?
questions.explanation: The primary key attributes are traditionally underlined in the schema representation.

questions.text: The rule stating that 'primary key attributes cannot have null values' is called:
questions.explanation: Entity integrity states that primary key attributes (PK) cannot have null values in any tuple.

questions.text: Which constraint involves specifying a relationship between tuples in two different relations?
questions.explanation: Referential integrity is a constraint involving two relations: a referencing relation and a referenced relation.

questions.text: A foreign key value can be either a primary key value in the referenced relation or:
questions.explanation: Referential integrity allows a foreign key to be null, provided it is not part of its own relation's primary key.

questions.text: In a relational schema diagram, referential integrity is displayed as a:
questions.explanation: Referential integrity constraints are displayed as directed arcs from the foreign key to the referenced primary key.

questions.text: Constraints like 'the maximum hours per employee is 56' are known as:
questions.explanation: Semantic integrity constraints are based on application semantics and cannot always be expressed by the basic model constraints.

questions.text: What does a 'relational database state' consist of?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The list of table names only

choices.label: B
choices.text: The union of all individual relation states in the database

choices.label: C
choices.text: A list of all possible values for each attribute

choices.label: D
choices.text: The SQL code used to create the tables

questions.explanation: A relational database state is a union of all individual relation states (populated tables) at a given time.

questions.text: Which basic update operation can violate any of the four constraints (Domain, Key, Entity Integrity, Referential Integrity)?
questions.explanation: INSERTing a new tuple can violate all mentioned constraints depending on the values provided.

questions.text: Which constraint is primarily at risk during a DELETE operation?
questions.explanation: DELETE may violate referential integrity if the primary key being deleted is still referenced by other tuples.

questions.text: The 'CASCADE' option for dealing with integrity violations means:

choices.label: B
choices.text: The user is informed but the change is made anyway.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The update/deletion is propagated to all referencing tuples.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Referencing values are set to a default value.

questions.explanation: The CASCADE option triggers additional updates so that the violation is corrected by propagating the change.

questions.text: What happens under the 'RESTRICT' (or REJECT) option during a violation?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The system automatically fixes the error.

questions.explanation: The RESTRICT option rejects the operation that causes the violation.

questions.text: Updating a primary key attribute is formally equivalent to which sequence of operations?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An INSERT followed by another INSERT

choices.label: B
choices.text: A DELETE followed by an INSERT

choices.label: C
choices.text: A MODIFY followed by a DELETE

choices.label: D
choices.text: A single MODIFY with no side effects

questions.explanation: Updating the primary key (PK) is similar to a DELETE of the old tuple followed by an INSERT of the new one.

questions.text: If you update an ordinary attribute that is neither a Primary Key nor a Foreign Key, which constraint could potentially be violated?
questions.explanation: Updating an ordinary attribute can only violate domain constraints (or NOT NULL constraints).

questions.text: Which SQL feature is mentioned as a way to express semantic integrity constraints?
questions.explanation: SQL-99 allows triggers and ASSERTIONS to express complex semantic integrity constraints.

questions.text: In the relation CAR(State, Reg#, SerialNo, Make, Model, Year), if {SerialNo} is a key, what is {SerialNo, Make} called?
questions.explanation: Any set of attributes that includes a key is a superkey, but it is not necessarily a key if it's not minimal.

questions.text: Which of the following is true about a 'Key' in a relation?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It must be the largest set of attributes in the relation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It can have duplicate values across rows.

questions.explanation: A key is defined as a minimal superkey; no subset of its attributes can maintain the uniqueness property.

questions.text: What is 'r(R)' formally called?
questions.explanation: r(R) represents a specific state or population of the relation R at a given time.

questions.text: Which concept is the relational model based on?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mathematical theory of sets and relations

questions.explanation: The relational model is based on the mathematical concept of a relation and the theory of sets.

questions.text: If \( t \) is a tuple in \( r(R) \), and \( R \) has attributes \( A_1, A_2, A_3 \), then \( t = <v_1, v_2, v_3> \) where each \( v_j \) must belong to:
questions.explanation: Each element \( v_j \) of a tuple must be a member of the domain of its corresponding attribute \( A_j \).

questions.text: In referential integrity, if tuple \( t_1 \) in relation \( R_1 \) references tuple \( t_2 \) in relation \( R_2 \), then:
questions.explanation: A tuple \( t_1 \) references \( t_2 \) if the foreign key values of \( t_1 \) match the primary key values of \( t_2 \).

questions.text: Which of the following describes the 'SET NULL' action for referential integrity violations?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The deletion of the referenced tuple is blocked.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The foreign keys of the referencing tuples are set to NULL.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A new tuple is created to replace the old one.

questions.explanation: The SET NULL option remedies a violation by setting the foreign key of the referencing tuples to NULL when the referenced tuple is deleted.

questions.text: A relational database schema \( S \) is formally defined as:

choices.label: B
choices.text: A set of relation schemas \( \{R_1, R_2, ..., R_n\} \) that belong to the same database

choices.label: D
choices.text: The data values currently in the tables

questions.explanation: A Relational Database Schema is a set of relation schemas that belong to the same database.

questions.text: What is the standard model used in practice (for database management systems) based on?
questions.explanation: In practice, there is a standard model based on SQL, which differs slightly from the formal relational model.

questions.text: Under the characteristics of relations, what is true about the ordering of attributes in a schema \( R(A_1, A_2, ..., A_n) \)?

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are considered ordered in this chapter's definition.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ordering is only required for primary keys.

questions.explanation: In the provided text, attributes in R and values in a tuple are considered ordered, though more general definitions exist where they are not.

questions.text: The set of all possible values that an attribute can take is its:
questions.explanation: A domain contains the set of all possible values the attribute can take.

questions.text: If a primary key consists of several attributes, entity integrity requires:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only the first attribute cannot be null.

choices.label: B
choices.text: At least one attribute must not be null.

choices.label: C
choices.text: None of the attributes in the primary key can be null.

questions.explanation: Entity integrity dictates that if the primary key has several attributes, null is not allowed in any of these attributes.

questions.text: A 'Subtuple' refers to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: A tuple in a different relation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A tuple containing only values of a specific subset of attributes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A tuple that is being deleted.

questions.explanation: Notation like \( t[A_u, A_v] \) refers to a subtuple containing values of specific attributes.
